title: NHS Workers Sing in a Play About Germs
summary: A fun musical play in London is about antibiotic resistance, which happens when germs stop responding to medicines. It stars 60 NHS healthcare workers like doctors and nurses who sing and act. The play tells the story of Sir Alexander Fleming, who discovered penicillin in 1928, and a junior doctor today. It shows how important it is to use antibiotics carefully so they keep working. The performers say singing makes them happy and gives them energy, even after long hospital shifts. The play has been performed in Edinburgh and at the United Nations to teach people about this big problem. It helps everyone understand why we need to protect our medicines from superbugs.

questions.correct_answer: When germs stop responding to medicines

background_read: Antibiotic resistance is a big problem where germs like bacteria become too strong for medicines to kill them. This happens if we use antibiotics too much or not correctly. Sir Alexander Fleming found penicillin, the first antibiotic, in 1928, and he warned that germs could become resistant. Today, doctors and scientists work hard to find new ways to fight germs. The NHS is the UK's health system that helps people stay healthy. Musicals are plays with music that can teach important lessons in a fun way, like this one about keeping medicines working.
Article_Structure: WHO: NHS healthcare workers and creators like Robin Hiley and Becky Hope-Palmer
WHAT: A musical about antibiotic resistance starring medical professionals
WHERE: London, Edinburgh, and the United Nations
WHY: To teach people about the danger of antibiotic resistance and promote singing for health

perspectives.description: The musical uses entertainment to teach about a serious health issue.
Performers find singing energizing and good for well-being.
